title: The Caddy

jokes.text: You throw a rock into a crowd, that's considered terrorism. But if you have a nice follow-through, you know, that's golf.
jokes.setup: Observation about golf balls hitting spectators at tournaments
jokes.punchline: The absurd double standard between throwing rocks and golf balls based purely on technique
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational comedy with perfect economy - captures absurdity of societal double standards in just two sentences. The terrorism/golf juxtaposition is unexpected and brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I don't understand about golf? Why is it okay to hit a golf ball into the crowd at a tournament, but if you threw a rock into the stands, you'd be arrested? It's the same thing! You're hitting people with projectiles. The only difference is one requires a follow-through.

jokes.text: It's a little white ball doing 100 miles an hour on a white background. 'I got it. I lost it. I got it. I lost it.'
jokes.setup: Observation about golf cameramen following the ball
jokes.punchline: The futility captured in the repetitive 'got it/lost it' mantra
jokes.explanation: Good observational setup with effective mimicry of the cameraman's internal monologue. The repetition creates the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You ever notice the cameramen at golf tournaments? They're following the ball the whole time. Got it, lost it. Got it, lost it. That's their whole job. Got it, lost it. Like they're playing some kind of game with the ball.

jokes.text: Take a ball out of your pocket. Throw it down. Who's gonna know where the hell it came from?
jokes.setup: Building on the cameraman bit
jokes.punchline: The suggestion to just fake it entirely since no one can track the ball anyway
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation of the golf coverage premise, though less sharp than the opening.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, if nobody can follow the ball anyway, why don't we just fake the whole thing? We'll have the cameraman just point at nothing for two hours.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's genius. Nobody would know the difference.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: We could be in a studio somewhere, the players could be playing checkers, and the announcers are just making it up as they go along.

jokes.text: How did you lock your keys in your car? How? Because I'm an idiot.
jokes.setup: Jerry asks the obvious question about George's car situation
jokes.punchline: George's matter-of-fact acceptance of his own stupidity
jokes.explanation: Perfect George moment - his self-awareness about his incompetence delivered with characteristic bluntness.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, how could you leave your car at the gas station?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't know. I just did.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You don't know? It's your car!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm not a details guy, Jerry. I never have been.

jokes.text: Just waiting for the membership to kick in.
jokes.setup: George signed up for Auto Club for free locksmith service
jokes.punchline: He's waiting for membership to activate while his car sits locked for days
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures George's warped logic - saving money while losing more through inaction. Very character-specific comedy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Jerry, this Auto Club is incredible. Free locksmith service. I just need to wait for the membership to activate.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: How long does that take?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: A few days. Maybe a week.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And your car is locked the whole time?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, but it's worth it for the peace of mind.

jokes.text: Calendar says winter, but the gods of spring are out.
jokes.setup: Jerry asks if Kramer is playing golf
jokes.punchline: Kramer's pretentious, poetic justification for winter golf
jokes.explanation: Classic Kramer pretension - turning simple weather into mythological poetry. Character-perfect delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're playing golf?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, why not? Winter golf, it's beautiful. The frost on the fairway, it's like a blanket of diamonds. And the ball, it travels further in the cold air. It's pure.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're insane.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, no, no. I'm a purist. Most people, they only play in the spring and summer. But the real golfer, he plays year-round. It's about the challenge, the elements.

jokes.text: I sneak in with Stan the Caddy. We go through the caddies' entrance.
jokes.setup: Explaining how he plays golf in winter
jokes.punchline: The idea of Kramer sneaking onto a closed course through service entrances
jokes.explanation: Sets up the Stan character while showing Kramer's willingness to bend rules for his hobbies.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I sneak onto the course through the service entrances. They can't stop me.

jokes.text: He thinks eventually I'll have a shot at making it big on the Senior Tour.
jokes.setup: Stan's advice has transformed Kramer's game
jokes.punchline: Kramer's delusional belief he could go professional
jokes.explanation: Typical Kramer grandiosity - taking minor improvement and projecting it to professional levels.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Jerry, I got it. Stan's advice has completely transformed my game. I'm gonna go pro.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Go pro? Kramer, you can barely break 90.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That's the old Kramer. This is the new Kramer. I'm a different man out there on the course.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You shot a 127 last week.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Jerry, I'm not listening to the negativity. I've already called three tournaments. I'm in.

jokes.text: Here's what you do. You bring a lightweight jacket. That way, the sun comes out, you play the jacket off the sweater.
jokes.setup: Jerry questions if George's pants are too cold for golf
jokes.punchline: Stan's overly strategic approach to basic clothing decisions
jokes.explanation: Establishes Stan's character as someone who overthinks everything, even simple wardrobe choices.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Your pants are too cold for golf.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Too cold? They're pants!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: They're thin pants, George. You need insulation.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I have a system. I wear the pants, then I put on a jacket. That's how it works.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not a system, that's just getting dressed.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It IS a system. It's MY system. I've been perfecting it for years.

jokes.text: Oh, great. It's the braless wonder.
jokes.setup: Elaine approaches, clearly without a bra
jokes.punchline: Direct, catty comment about Elaine's appearance
jokes.explanation: Sets up the bra subplot but the humor is fairly basic - just a snide comment.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown woman
dialogue.line: Is it cold in here, or did you just get excited?

jokes.text: Well, I hope you can get me a gift anyway.
jokes.setup: Elaine says she's too busy to attend Sue Ellen's birthday party
jokes.punchline: Sue Ellen's shameless expectation that she still deserves a gift
jokes.explanation: Perfect entitlement moment that establishes Sue Ellen's character efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I just can't make it to your party. I'm swamped at work.

dialogue.speaker: Sue Ellen
dialogue.line: Well, you're still getting me a gift, right?

jokes.text: So keep it under your hat.
jokes.setup: Wilhelm tells George about potential promotion
jokes.punchline: The irony that George won't be around to keep anything secret
jokes.explanation: Situational irony that works better on rewatch when you know George will be absent.

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: George, I'm giving you a promotion. You'll be vice president of the Eastern Division.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Vice president? That's wonderful!

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: But you must promise me one thing - you keep this confidential. Not a word to anyone until I make the official announcement.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Of course, Mr. Wilhelm. My lips are sealed.

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: Good. Because if this leaks out before I'm ready, there will be consequences.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You can count on me, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: You're fired, Costanza.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? Why?

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: I don't like the way you looked at me. Now get out of my office.

jokes.text: I can't understand why they haven't won a pennant in 15 years.
jokes.setup: George explains his accidental success at work
jokes.punchline: Jerry's deadpan commentary on Yankees management quality
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry observation - using George's situation to comment on organizational dysfunction.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what you're telling me is that by doing nothing, by being completely inert, you've become successful at your job?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, that's actually the same management philosophy the Yankees have been using for the last fifteen years.

jokes.text: Locking your keys in your car is the best career move you ever made.
jokes.setup: George's accidental workplace success story
jokes.punchline: The absurd notion that incompetence led to career advancement
jokes.explanation: Brilliant encapsulation of the George Costanza experience - failure leading to success through misunderstanding.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm out there, Jerry, and I'm lovin' every minute of it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What happened?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I went back to my apartment, I got on the computer, I'm looking at old-school baseball movies. I'm thinking about the good times. I'm thinking about how life used to be. And I said to myself, 'Why did I ever stop doing this?' So I decided I'm gonna start doing it again.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you mean you're gonna start doing it again?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I mean, I'm gonna start approaching women the way I used to. I used to have a little system, it worked pretty well. I would call women up, I would say things that made them laugh. They seemed to like that.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what changed?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't know. Life. Confidence. Stuff. But you know what? It all just came back to me. Because I did something today that I haven't done in a long time. I did it because I felt like it. And you know what? It worked.

jokes.text: This woman has never, not once ever, as long as I have known her... worn a bra.
jokes.setup: Elaine describing Sue Ellen Mischke
jokes.punchline: The emphatic way she describes this as a lifelong pattern
jokes.explanation: The repetitive emphasis creates comedy through Elaine's obvious obsession with this detail.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: She's been wearing the same bra since high school!

jokes.text: She's your Lex Luthor.
jokes.setup: Elaine describes her history with Sue Ellen
jokes.punchline: Jerry's perfect pop culture comparison for Elaine's nemesis
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character insight - distills the entire relationship into three words with a brilliant pop culture reference.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So this woman is basically your Lex Luthor.

jokes.text: Have I ever bought you a jockstrap as a gift?
jokes.setup: Elaine claims the bra might just be seen as a gift
jokes.punchline: Jerry's perfect gender-flipped analogy
jokes.explanation: Sharp analogy that exposes the absurdity of Elaine's rationalization with perfect parallel logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A gift? If I gave a woman a bra, she'd think I was saying, 'Hey, I've noticed your breasts. Here, strap 'em in.'

jokes.text: My presence in that office can only hurt my chances.
jokes.setup: Jerry questions taking time off during potential promotion
jokes.punchline: George's backwards logic about avoiding work to get promoted
jokes.explanation: Pure George logic - turning conventional wisdom completely upside down with twisted reasoning.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're not going to take any time off?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No. No time off. If I don't take time off, if I show up every single day, they have to promote me. It's like a catch-22. They have no choice.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not a catch-22.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know, I know. But it's close.

jokes.text: It's not a top. It's a bra.
jokes.setup: Sue Ellen thanks Elaine for her 'lovely gift'
jokes.punchline: Elaine's mortified clarification
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective cringe moment as Elaine's plan backfires immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Sue Ellen
dialogue.line: Thank you so much for the lovely gift.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, that wasn't a gift. That was just something I didn't want anymore.

jokes.text: Oh, I know.
jokes.setup: Elaine's mortified correction about the bra
jokes.punchline: Sue Ellen's casual acknowledgment that she knows exactly what it is
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Sue Ellen's nonchalant response shows she's completely unashamed and turning the tables on Elaine.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's not a bra, it's a... it's a... bra

dialogue.speaker: Sue Ellen
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know what a bra is.

jokes.text: All right, fine. I'll drive the three hours each way and take them off myself.
jokes.setup: Jerry complains about George's requests
jokes.punchline: George's threat to drive six hours total to remove fliers
jokes.explanation: Classic George guilt trip with absurd proportions - threatening massive inconvenience to avoid asking for help.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You want me to drive six hours total to remove some fliers?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, what do you want me to do? I can't drive six hours.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I have to do it?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: If you don't, I'm never speaking to you again.

jokes.text: I could have said just about anything.
jokes.setup: Kramer immediately agrees to go to the Bronx
jokes.punchline: Jerry's observation about Kramer's indiscriminate enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Nice meta moment where Jerry comments on the show's own character dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, with you it's always yes. Yes to everything. Someone says 'Let's go to the Bronx,' you say yes. You don't even think about it.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why not? Because it's the Bronx!

jokes.text: Oh, my God, Kramer, is that woman just wearing a bra?
jokes.setup: Jerry and Kramer washing the car
jokes.punchline: Jerry spots Sue Ellen in her bra-as-top
jokes.explanation: Setup for the car crash - Jerry's shock at seeing the result of Elaine's gift.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh my God!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What? What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Don't look now, but isn't that Sue Ellen Mischke?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Where?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Over there, by the corner.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, that's her. What's she wearing?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Is that a bra?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It looks like a bra.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She's wearing a bra as a top!

jokes.text: Oh, mama.
jokes.setup: Kramer sees Sue Ellen in the bra
jokes.punchline: Kramer's lustful reaction
jokes.explanation: Simple character reaction that motivates the crash - Kramer being Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Hello!

jokes.text: She's wearing it as a top.
jokes.setup: Learning about the crash and Sue Ellen's outfit
jokes.punchline: Elaine's horror at how her gift backfired
jokes.explanation: Moment of realization for Elaine that her petty revenge has had unintended consequences.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I can't believe this. I gave her that blouse as a gift and now she's dead!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Elaine, you can't blame yourself. It's not your fault she wore it in a plane crash.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: But that blouse was so bright and colorful. What if the pilot was distracted? What if he couldn't see the runway because he was looking at her outfit?

jokes.text: She's a menace to society.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Sue Ellen wearing bra as top
jokes.punchline: Jerry's dramatic overstatement of the social threat
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - treating a fashion choice as a public safety issue.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's like she's declaring war on fabrics. I mean, we've come to an understanding with the bra industry, okay? It stays under, we stay out. But now she's breaking the treaty.

jokes.text: Your dreams have been shattered. Somebody's gotta be held accountable.
jokes.setup: Jackie encouraging Kramer to sue
jokes.punchline: The melodramatic language for a minor injury
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jackie Chiles character introduction - overwrought legal melodrama for trivial matters.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: This is a travesty! A miscarriage of justice! You have been wronged, my friend. The pain and suffering you have endured is immeasurable. We will take this case all the way to the Supreme Court if we have to!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It's just a paper cut.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: A paper cut?! A PAPER CUT?! Do you know what a paper cut can lead to? Infection! Gangrene! Amputation! We're talking about a multi-million dollar settlement here!

jokes.text: It's lewd, lascivious, salacious, outrageous.
jokes.setup: Jackie describing Sue Ellen's outfit
jokes.punchline: The alliterative string of synonyms delivered with legal gravitas
jokes.explanation: Classic Jackie Chiles moment - the rhythmic legal terminology delivered with perfect character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: This outfit is a travesty, a mockery, a farce! It's a travesty, a mockery, a farce of a mockery of a travesty!

jokes.text: Oh Henry!? That's one of our top-selling candy bars. It's got chocolate, peanuts, nougat. It's delicious, scrumptious, outstanding.
jokes.setup: Learning Sue Ellen is Oh Henry! heiress
jokes.punchline: Jackie's sudden candy bar expertise and enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Jackie's character trait of getting distracted by tangential details, delivered with legal-level intensity.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: Oh Henry! Oh Henry! Oh Henry! Do you know what this means? This is a very significant confection. The nougat-to-caramel ratio is impeccable. I've been eating these bars for thirty years and I never knew they were made by royalty!

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: We need to get you the best representation. This is a major candy situation. I'm talking serious chocolate bar jurisdiction here.

jokes.text: Like taking candy from a baby.
jokes.setup: Asked if they have a case against the candy bar heiress
jokes.punchline: The perfectly appropriate idiom given the candy bar connection
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that works on multiple levels - legal confidence and literal candy connection. Perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: We have no case. You can't get blood out of a turnip.

jokes.text: Jerry took the fliers off the car. I got the whole thing covered.
jokes.setup: Wilhelm worrying about the car situation
jokes.punchline: George's false confidence about covering his tracks
jokes.explanation: Dramatic irony since we know the car is crashed and bloody - works better on rewatch.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Don't worry about it. I got it all figured out. I'll just tell them the car got stolen. Insurance will cover it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm serious. I've thought this through. I'll file a police report, say I parked it on the street, came back and it was gone.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? It's foolproof. They're not gonna check anything. They just pay out.

jokes.text: Obviously, he was in some sort of a terrible car accident... and trouper that he is, he tried to make it into work, sir.
jokes.setup: Wilhelm reports on George's crashed, bloody car
jokes.punchline: Wilhelm's dramatic misinterpretation of the evidence
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony - Wilhelm creating a heroic narrative from completely wrong evidence.

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: This car, it has been through something terrible. The blood on the seats, the twisted metal, the shattered glass - it tells a story of violence and suffering.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wilhelm, that's just ketchup and a fender bender.

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: A fender bender? No, no. This is a crime scene. A struggle between man and machine. Perhaps a wild animal was involved.

jokes.text: Find him, Wilhelm.
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner's concern for missing George
jokes.punchline: The military-style urgency for finding a mediocre employee
jokes.explanation: Establishes Steinbrenner's character - treating minor employee issues with wartime intensity.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Where's Costanza? I want him found immediately. I don't care if we have to drag the Hudson River, I want results!

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Sir, he's probably just running late...

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Running late? RUNNING LATE?! This is a code red situation! Get me the National Guard, the Coast Guard, and somebody call the Russians — they know how to find people!

jokes.text: He's dead. Costanza's dead.
jokes.setup: Wilhelm reports they can't find George anywhere
jokes.punchline: Steinbrenner immediately jumping to death conclusion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Steinbrenner dramatic overreaction - no middle ground between missing and dead.

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: We can't find George anywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: He's dead. George is dead.

jokes.text: We can't grieve forever. Gotta get back to business.
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner dramatically mourning George
jokes.punchline: Immediately pivoting to business practicality
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Steinbrenner's emotional whiplash from grief to business mode.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: George is gone. A good man. A good employee. I will miss him.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Now, who's going to take over his desk?

jokes.text: You're just out for sex! You're just out for money!
jokes.setup: Jerry and Kramer arguing about the lawsuit
jokes.punchline: Their simultaneous accusations revealing their true motivations
jokes.explanation: Perfect symmetrical argument where both characters expose each other's shallow motivations.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're the one who started this whole thing!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Me? You're just trying to cover your tracks!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I only care about what's fair!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Fair? You care about the money!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, you care about being right!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: At least I'm honest about it!

jokes.text: What the hell did you trade Jay Buhner for? He had 30 home runs and over 100 RBls last year. He's got a rocket for an arm. You don't know what the hell you're doing!
jokes.setup: Frank learning of George's 'death' from Steinbrenner
jokes.punchline: Frank immediately attacking Steinbrenner's baseball decisions instead of grieving
jokes.explanation: Legendary Seinfeld moment - Frank's grief expressing itself through baseball statistics. Perfect character writing and completely unexpected response.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Frank, I have some terrible news. Your son, George, he's dead.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What?! Dead?!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: How could you let this happen?! He was my boy! My only son!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: And another thing - those trades you made last season were a disaster! Trading away Rodriguez for nothing?! What were you thinking?!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: You destroy my son's life with your incompetence, and you destroy the team too!

jokes.text: They could use the break.
jokes.setup: George asks if he should tell his parents he's alive
jokes.punchline: Jerry's dark suggestion that his parents might prefer thinking he's dead
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark Jerry observation delivered with perfect deadpan economy. Captures the George family dysfunction perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Should I tell my parents I'm alive?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know, they might be happier thinking you're dead.

jokes.text: I managed to survive... on grubs and puddle water until a kindly old gentleman picked me up.
jokes.setup: George explaining his absence to Steinbrenner
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous survival story to explain a few days away
jokes.explanation: Classic George elaborate lie - turning a vacation into a survival epic.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Mr. Steinbrenner, I can explain my absence. I was in the Amazon rainforest studying the mating habits of the jaguar. My research team and I were attacked by a swarm of bullet ants. I had to fashion a makeshift tourniquet from vines and navigate back to civilization using only the stars and my knowledge of osmosis.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Costanza, that's the most ridiculous thing I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's all true, sir. Every word.

jokes.text: Gotta admit, I never tasted one of those.
jokes.setup: George mentions surviving on grubs
jokes.punchline: Steinbrenner's casual curiosity about eating insects
jokes.explanation: Perfect Steinbrenner character moment - getting distracted by culinary curiosity during serious conversation.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I could survive on grubs.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Grubs? What kind of grubs?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, grubs. Insects.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: And they're... edible?

jokes.text: Not as long as I'm running the show!
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner's speech about chaos not working for the Yankees
jokes.punchline: The dramatic conclusion about maintaining order
jokes.explanation: Good Steinbrenner character moment but fairly standard authoritarian speech pattern.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Chaos doesn't work for the Yankees. We need order, we need discipline, we need structure. That's what wins championships. That's what I've built here. And I'm not going to let some fly-by-night operation come in here and destroy everything I've worked for.

jokes.text: And she was beautiful in that bra. I'm crazy about her. I love her whole free-swinging, freewheeling attitude.
jokes.setup: Jerry forced to testify about seeing Sue Ellen
jokes.punchline: Jerry's complete romantic capitulation on the witness stand
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment as Jerry completely abandons his friend's case for romantic reasons.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Mr. Seinfeld, you were present at the incident in question?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yes, Your Honor.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: And you saw the defendant, Sue Ellen Mischke?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I did. She was... she looked really good that day.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Mr. Seinfeld, please stick to the facts.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, she had this dress on, and her hair was perfect. I mean, if you're going to accuse someone, at least they should look like that.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: This is a court of law, not a dating service.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know, but I'm just being honest. Whatever she did, I'm willing to overlook it.

jokes.text: Have her try on the bra. See if it fits.
jokes.setup: Jackie asks Stan for legal advice
jokes.punchline: Stan applying golf strategy to courtroom tactics
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character integration - Stan treating the courtroom like a golf course with strategic advice.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie
dialogue.line: Stan, I need legal advice. What should I do?

dialogue.speaker: Stan
dialogue.line: Well, the key is to keep your head down, stay focused, and remember: it's all about the follow-through.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie
dialogue.line: That's your legal strategy?

dialogue.speaker: Stan
dialogue.line: Works on the golf course. Why shouldn't it work in court?

jokes.text: You damn fools! Look at that. We got nothing now. Nothing. I've been practicing law for 25 years, you're listening to a caddy.
jokes.setup: The bra doesn't fit over Sue Ellen's leotard
jokes.punchline: Jackie's complete meltdown about taking advice from a caddy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jackie Chiles meltdown - professional pride colliding with the absurdity of the situation.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: This is unconscionable! You took the advice of a caddy? A CADDY?! Do you know what a caddy is? A caddy is a guy who carries clubs! I have gone to school for eight years to get my law degree, and you're taking tips from a man who makes his living hauling sticks!

jokes.text: A bra's gotta fit right up against a person's skin. Like a glove!
jokes.setup: Jackie realizes the demonstration was flawed
jokes.punchline: The passionate legal argument about proper bra fitting, ending with the OJ glove reference
jokes.explanation: Brilliant pop culture reference tying Jackie to the OJ trial while maintaining character voice. Perfect blend of topical and timeless.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: Your Honor, the demonstration was fundamentally flawed. You cannot simply take a bra off a woman and expect it to fit properly on another woman. Every woman is different! The architecture is different, the support system is different.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: This is not unlike the glove situation. If it doesn't fit, you must acquit!

jokes.text: Oh, I don't think you'll have any trouble getting a table.
jokes.setup: Sue Ellen invites Elaine to lunch despite having reservations
jokes.punchline: Elaine's sarcastic comment about Sue Ellen's attention-getting outfit
jokes.explanation: Nice closing beat that acknowledges the visual comedy of Sue Ellen's continued bra-wearing.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's a great outfit. Really subtle. I'm sure nobody will notice you in that.

tmdb_overview: Kramer sues Elaine's friend for sinking his golf career; George's apparent work ethic impresses his bosses.
insight: Caddy subplot escalates absurdly while character comedy sustains 1.8 jokes per minute.
